coredata.dc:title: 3D Nanomaterials: The future of industrial, biological, and environmental applications
coredata.dc:creator: Verma, Chandrabhan
Berdimurodov, Elyor
Verma, Dakeshwar K.
Berdimuradov, Khasan
Alfantazi, Akram
Hussain, Chaudhery Mustansar
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis and application of 3D nanomaterials (3D NMs) are gaining particular attention as they contribute more than 85% to the nanofamily. They are associated with salient features, including high specific surface area, dispensability, and crystallinity. They are widely used for different industrial, biological, and environmental purposes. They are widely used for biomedical, energy storage, sensing and biosensing, pollutants treatment, catalysis, tribology, corrosion protection, etc. Their sensing, biosensing, and other applications are associated with several advantageous properties, such as long-term stability, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, recyclability, and high selectivity. They have been widely used in sensing various gases, volatile compounds, and pesticides. They have also been used as nanofillers in polymer-based anticorrosive coatings. The 3D NMs raise the effectiveness and durability of coating performance by repairing the surface cracks and micropores. The 3D NMs are extensively used for decontaminating pollutants such as dyes, drugs, and phenolic compounds due to their practicality, recycling, and better capacity. The present review article describes the recent developments in the synthesis and use of 3D NMs. The challenges and future opportunities of 3D NMs in different fields are also described. To the best of our knowledge, this will be the first report that describes all aspects of 3D NMs.

Basics of 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides for gas sensing.

row.entry: At low temperatures, Pt-doped 3D SnO2 nanomaterials are highly sensitive materials for alcohol vapours. The oxygen atoms are mainly responsible for gas detection.
3D Fe2O3/Fe-honeycomb-like N-doped graphitic carbon nanomaterial (3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC)

The low values of NO2 gas were accurately detected by the 3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC at room temperatures. The selectivity and sensitivity of this material for gas sensing are also very high. The sensitivity concentrations of NO2 gas on the 3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC detection was 25.48–100 ppm at 2.13 s. The sensitive concentration limit is 10 ppb. The recycling time is 11.73 s and stability is 2 months.
Si-doped 3D WO3 nanowires interwoven into 3D mesoporous superstructures (Pt/Si-WO3 NWIMSs)

At 100 °C, the ethanol sensing for Pt/Si-WO3 NWIMSs was very high: long-term stability, excellent selectivity, fast response–recovery speed (17–7 s), low detection limit (0.5 ppm), high sensitivity (S = 93 vs 50 ppm).

At 285 °C, the 3D aloe-like SnO2 sense the 50 ppb ethanol, confirming the ultrasensitivity of 3D aloe-like SnO2. The thinner structure and less agglomeration are mainly responsible for the sensing degree of this 3D material.

The ethanol sensing of flower-like 3D ZnO nanostructures was checked at 500 °C. In this sensing, the formation of active ethoxy and oxygen promotes ethanol detection. In the atmosphere, the 3D ZnO nanostructures reacted with the N2 to form the ZnO–N2, which is also responsible for ethanol detection.
The TEA detection ability of 3D Ag-ZnO was very high at 100 ppm and more effective than other ZnO-based sensors. Moreover, the sensing properties of 3D Ag-ZnO were reduced at high temperatures.
3D Co3O4@CF is a super-sensing agent for ethanol detection. Its sensing limit is 15 ppm at 320 °C. It also has high sensitivity, selectivity, fast response and recovery time.
CO gas (<10 ppm) was effectively detected by the 3D ZnO/RGO. It is a fast response and recovery time of 10 sec. It works effectively at 200 °C.
3D Ag/In2O3 can sense the HCHO gas at 20 ppm. Its selectivity is high, recovery time is 14 s and fast response time is 0.9 s, respectively.
Toluene, xylene, acetone, ethyl-acetate and ethanol

3D flower-like CuO is an excellent sensor for toluene, xylene, acetone, ethyl-acetate and ethanol gases at 1000 ppm. Rg/Ra = 2.8 for toluene; Rg/Ra = 4 for ethanol; Rg/Ra = 4.6 for ethyl-acetate. This material is recyclable, easily recovered and fast response in gas sensing.
3D flower-like TiO2-based nanostructures decorated with SnO2
3D TiO2 - SnO2 is an excellent sensing agent for CO(CH3)2 at 8 ppm and 308 °C. This material is more stable, and selective. The response time is around 1–5 s.
3D ZnSnO3 effectively detected the acetone and ethanol at 0.18 s and 0.26 s, respectively at 50 ppb (detection limit).
3D SnO2 flower-like nanomaterials with Pt nanomaterials

The environmental friendly methods were used to prepare 3D SnO2 / Pt nanomaterials. This material is recyclable, recovery, rapidly response, more selective and low-cost at sensing ethanol at low concentrations. The synergic electronic interaction between Pt NPs and SnO2 is mainly responsible for gas sensing.

3D hierarchically porous ZnO nanostructures are very fast in response and have a higher sensitivity to CO sensing. AuNP-functionalized 3D ZnO nanostructures are effective for gas detection, photocatalysis and low-temperature CO oxidation.
3D Assembly of Cu₂O Decorated WS₂ Nanomaterial
The H2S sensing of this nanomaterial is 13 times higher than the WS2 materials. The sensing property of this 3D metal oxide was increased when the humidity rise to 50%.

3D ZnO NPs are aerogel. It can detect the NO2 gas at low concentrations with the highest accuracy. The macroporous and conductivity are responsible for gas sensing. The 3D ZnO NPs are repeatable, recyclable, fast-response, eco-friendly fabrication, lightweight and low-cost in the detection of gas at room temperature.

3D hierarchical SnO2 is superior selectivity, fast response–recovery characteristics, and good reproducibility adding a high response for n-butanol gas at ppm degrees. Its porous structure promotes gas sensing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Basics of 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides for biosensing.

row.entry: 3D Ho2O3 nanomaterials effectively detect the moisturizing effect of skin care products, body surface humidity and human breathing vital signs. Its recovery time is 6 s and its response time is 0.3 s. It is recyclable, reproducible and stable for human humidity sensing.

The sensitive selectivity is high (195 mA M−1 cm−2), with two wide linear ranges (0.001–5.2 mM, 5.2–17.1 mM) at a working voltage of 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in an alkaline solution. The glucose sensing of this material appeared at the low-onset potential (0.25 V) in 0.1 M NaOH solution.
Glucose, glucose solution containing 1 mM ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and dopamine (DA)
The Au-3D ZnO nanomaterial is a super glucose sensor and the sensing limit is 0.02 mM. It can sense the glucose in the 1–20 mM ranges.
H2O2 in the clinical, pharmaceutical and biochemical applications
3D MnO2 nanosheets/carbon foam composite effectively sense the H2O2 from the clinical, pharmaceutical and biochemical samples.
3D Large-Scale TiO2 nanorods Scaffold decorated by Ag nanoparticles
The detection limit of 3D Ag/TiO2 is 1 × 10–12 M for MG.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D nanomaterials based 3D transition metal oxides in organic dyes removal.

row.entry: D-HPT more effectively remove the target dye. The adsorption capacitance is high. The 24 ppm dye was removed with 40% efficiency.
3D-TiO2@Hierarchical Porous Graphene Aerogels (HPGA), (m3D-T) and magnetic nanocomposites, 3D-TiO2 (b-3D-T) magnetic nanomaterials
The selected 3D nanomaterials are more selective and super adsorbents for BPA. The maximal adsorption efficiency was 90% at 4 h.
3D Co/CoOx Nanoparticles with decorated N-doped carbon nanofiber electrodes
The Co/CoOx nanomaterials were more effective for the adsorption of AO7 dye. The maximum decolourization degree is over 85% (0.1 mM AO7) solution after 10 cycles (400 min) of degradation.
The maximum 1261.97 mg/l methyl orange dye was adsorbed on the surface of 3D Zn-Co-LDH. The adsorption rate is faster than the traditional ones.
The uniqueness of TiO2 nanoflowers (NFs) with a coarse texture and arranged structures demonstrates higher photocatalytic activity.
The maximum sorption degree of this material for Congo red dye is 271 mg/g.
The decolourization degree of 3D TiO2 was high for Rhodamine B (RhB) dye under photocatalytic degradation.
The decolourization degree of 3D CeO2 / TiO2 was high with 99.89% for Rhodamine B (RhB) dye under photocatalytic degradation. The adsorption peak is 579 nm after 8 h.
The oil and soluble dye were favourably adsorbed on the surface of MXene/O-MWCNT.
Methyl orange (MO) and Congo red (CR), dyes
The decolourization rate of MO and CR dyes on the surface of 3D Co-Doped SnO2 nanomaterial is high (1.036 min−1).

Methyl orange, fuchsin basic, gentian violet, safranine T, rhodamine B and
methylene blue.
The adsorption degree of 3D Fe3O4@1 for methyl orange, fuchsin basic, gentian violet, safranine T, rhodamine B and methylene blue is over 96%.
The 3D multifunctional Fe3O4 nanoparticles were super adsorbent for cationic dye with over 96% efficiency (2 min). This material was flexible after 10cycles, with high stability and selectivity.
3D self-assembly of porous α-FeOOH and α-Fe2O3 hierarchical nanomaterials

The neutral dye adsorption capacitance of these 3D α-FeOOH and α-Fe2O3 hierarchical nanomaterials were investigated in wastewater. It is clear from the obtained results that the 3D α-FeOOH and α-Fe2O3 hierarchical nanomaterials effectively adsorbed the neutral dyes from the wastewater.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 3D nanomaterials based 3D transition metal oxides in phenolic compound removal.

row.entry: 3D Mn3O4-H/PMS nanomaterial has a good catalytically effect on the degradation of phenol. The pseudo-first-order rate law was used to describe the photodegradation of phenol. It is indicated that the photodegradation of phenol on the surface of 3D Mn3O4-H/PMS nanomaterial required higher energy (Activation energy is 41.6 kJ/mol).

The phenol was degraded by the 3D BioMnOx and 3D α-Mn2O3 with 100% efficiency after 40 min. It is also noted that the 3D BioMnOx and 3D α-Mn2O3 has also photo catalytically impacts on the photodegradation of tetracycline with the 99.4% after 1 h. The 3D BioMnOx was more efficient than the 3D α-Mn2O3 in the photodegradation of phenolic compounds.

This material effectively insulates the 4-nitro-phenol (4-NP), 2,6-dinitro-phenol (2,6-DNP) and 2-nitro-phenol (2-NP) with a strong sodium borohydride (NaBH4) reducing agent from the wastewater. The reduction rate is 1.036 min−1.

The 3D γ-M nO2@ ZnFe2O4/rGO nanohybrid effectively degraded the phenol-based compounds by photo-catalytically acting under the light irritation. The 85% of 20 ppm of phenol solution was degraded with the 3D γ-M nO2@ ZnFe2O4/rGO nanohybrid at 25 °C after 0.5 h.

The rGO/SnO2 nanomaterial effectively senses and degraded the phenol compounds. This material is thermodynamically stable, has repeatability and excellent sensitivity for 10–80 ppb concentration ranges.
The phenol compounds in wastewater were favourably removed with the 3D CdS/CeO2.

The 50 mg/L phenol compounds were significantly removed to a high degree by the NiCo2O4/PMS nanomaterial surface for 25 min. This phenol removal is a photodegradation process and its degradation constant is 0.19651 min−1, which is 6.2 times higher than that of the Co3O4/PMS system.
The 1% mole ratio of Ce-doped ZnO composites removed 69.6% of phenol from wastewater for 120 min.

3D Fe/γ-Al2O3 nanomaterials effectively removed the phenol from the hydrogen peroxide solution. The phenol removal depends on the structural, molecular, and activation centres of porous. The metal–oxygen bonds also affect the phenol adsorption, because, the phenol interacted with the metal–oxygen bonds.

The 3D-hierarchically structured MnO2 is an excellent agent in the catalytic degradation of phenol under light irritation. This degradation process required more activated energy (25.3 kJ/mol). In this research, electron paramagnetic spectroscopy was used to analyze the photodegradation mechanism of phenol on the studied nanomaterial surface, suggesting that the

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis and application of 3D nanomaterials (3D NMs) are gaining particular attention as they contribute more than 85% to the nanofamily. They are associated with salient features, including high specific surface area, dispensability, and crystallinity. They are widely used for different industrial, biological, and environmental purposes. They are widely used for biomedical, energy storage, sensing and biosensing, pollutants treatment, catalysis, tribology, corrosion protection, etc. Their sensing, biosensing, and other applications are associated with several advantageous properties, such as long-term stability, excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, recyclability, and high selectivity. They have been widely used in sensing various gases, volatile compounds, and pesticides. They have also been used as nanofillers in polymer-based anticorrosive coatings. The 3D NMs raise the effectiveness and durability of coating performance by repairing the surface cracks and micropores. The 3D NMs are extensively used for decontaminating pollutants such as dyes, drugs, and phenolic compounds due to their practicality, recycling, and better capacity. The present review article describes the recent developments in the synthesis and use of 3D NMs. The challenges and future opportunities of 3D NMs in different fields are also described. To the best of our knowledge, this will be the first report that describes all aspects of 3D NMs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials (NMs) or nanoscale materials (NSMs) are an exciting and relatively new class of materials that have a length array of nearly 1 to 100 nm [1,2]. This comprises pieces of nano-objects and nanostructured materials that are pieces of materials and materials with external surfaces or internal structures in nanoscale dimensions [1,2]. The European Commission (EC) approved the following definition of a nanomaterial: “A natural, incidental or manufactured material containing particles, in an unbound state or as an aggregate or as an agglomerate and for 50% or more of the particles in the number size distribution, one or more external dimensions is in the size range 1 nm – 100 nm [3,4]. In specific cases and where warranted by concerns for the environment, health, safety or competitiveness, the number size distribution threshold of 50% may be replaced by a threshold between 1% and 50%”. Nanoscale materials display excellent chemical, physical, mechanical, and catalytic properties, principally due to their vast superficial area at very small length scales [5–7]. Enormous attention has been paid to nanoscale structure synthesis, functionalization, characterization and applications [7–10]. The nanoscale materials can be broadly classified as organic-, inorganic- or mixed (composite)-types. Based on their dimension, the nanoscale structures can be classified as 0D, 1D, 2D, and 3D (Fig. 1 ) [11].
Structural advantages of 3D nanomaterials in comparison with 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials.
It was found a discussion comparing the structural advantages of 3D nanomaterials versus 0D, 1D, and 2D nanomaterials [15–17]:
0D nanomaterials like nanoparticles have a high ratio of surface atoms to bulk atoms, but limited surface area accessibility since the particles tend to aggregate.
1D nanomaterials like nanowires provide greater accessible surface area due to their elongated structure. However, they still tend to bundle together limiting exposure of their surface.
2D nanomaterials like graphene offer a very high theoretical surface area. However, the 2D sheets tend to restack on each other, reducing accessible surface area.
In contrast, 3D nanomaterials have structures containing interconnected macro/mesopores that prevent aggregation and restacking. This provides the highest accessible surface area among nanomaterials.

Additional advantages of 3D nanomaterials [21–26]:
-
Macro/mesoporous channels allow exposure of inner surfaces and access for molecules/ions into the interior void spaces. This enables applications like catalysis, drug delivery, sensing, etc.
-
The porous structure provides a high loading capacity for functional components like catalyst particles or drugs.
-
The pores create confinement effects that can modulate chemical/physical properties.
-
The inner void space allows mass transport through the 3D structure.
-
The 3D morphology improves mechanical stability compared to 1D/2D nanomaterials.

Additional points comparing the structural advantages of 3D nanomaterials versus other dimensionalities:
-
Surface chemistry: The large surface area of 3D nanomaterials allows greater ability to tailor the surface chemistry through functionalization. More binding sites are available for modifying with target molecules.
-
Controlled dimensionality: 3D nanomaterials can be designed with controlled macro/mesoporosity and pore interconnectivity. This allows tuning of the 3D structure for desired properties.
-
Morphological versatility: 3D nanomaterials can be synthesized in various shapes like spheres, tubes, gyroids, etc. This allows the optimization of shapes for different applications.
-
Hybrid structures: The 3D porous network enables incorporation of multiple components to create hybrid nanomaterials. For example, combining 3D graphene with metal nanoparticles.
-
Defect engineering: The abundance of surface area in 3D nanomaterials provides more opportunity to engineer defects to modulate reactivity.
-
Synergistic effects: Combining 3D nanomaterials with other nanomaterials like 0D, 1D, or 2D can provide synergistic effects from the complementary structures.
-
Scalability: Techniques like templating allow scalable production of 3D nanostructures with consistent features like pore size.
-
Biocompatibility: The high porosity and surface area of some 3D nanomaterials provides good biocompatibility for bioapplications.

In summary, the unique 3D porous architecture of these nanomaterials imparts tremendous advantages of large accessible surface area, tunable void spaces, enhanced mass transport, high loading capacity, and excellent mechanical stability. These benefits enable diverse applications across many fields. Additionally, the unique architectural advantages of 3D nanomaterials impart greater structural control, multifunctionality, synergy, processability, and performance optimization compared to the other dimensionalities of nanomaterials.

The nanoscale materials possess potential applications in various engineering and science fields, including filters, paints, and lubricant additives [12–14]. Many nanoscale materials acquire enzyme-like properties and are used as nanonzymes in healthcare, such as antifouling, bioimaging, tumour diagnosis, biosensing, etc. [15,16]. The nanoscale materials are also extensively used to develop specific biological (e.g. virus), water, and air filters [17,18]. These materials are also used as highly effective insulators. Nanoscale materials are widely used as catalysts for various chemical transformations because of their high surface areas [19,20]. The relative distribution of nanoscale materials in various industry branches and their relative share are presented in Fig. 2 (a). Careful observation suggests that the major five industry branches where nanoscale materials acquire substantial attention are electronics, medicine, cosmetics, construction, and textile [21–23]. Other major branches include automotive, environment, renewable energies, and food. The relative distribution of 0D, 1D, 2D and 3D nanoscale materials is diagrammatically illustrated in Fig. 2(b) [24]. Among the different nanoscale materials, 3D nanomaterials constitute 85%. Therefore, the development and application of 3D nanoscale materials acquired significant attention. The present review describes the recent developments in the energy, environmental, biomedical, anticorrosive, and other emerging applications of 3D nanoscale materials.

The 3D metal oxides (MOs), especially transition metal oxides (TMOs) have attracted significant attention because of their remarkable physicochemical properties, ease, cost-effective synthesis, and non-toxic nature [25,26]. MOs are highly crystalline solids that contain metal cations and oxide anions [27,28]. MOs react with water or acids to form bases or salts, respectively. Alkali metals alkaline earth metals form three-type of oxides, namely, oxides, peroxides, and superoxides containing oxide (O2–), peroxide (O2 2–), and superoxide (O2 –) ions, respectively. The oxides, peroxides, and superoxides of alkali metals can be presented as M2O, M2O2, and MO2, respectively. Transition metal oxides (TMOs) are the most significant class of MOs. They comprise transition metal cations and oxygen anions [29,30]. They possess potential industrial and biological applications [31,32]. Their properties can be suitably tailored by varying their surface properties and functionalization. The ionic radius of oxygen anion is usually greater as compared to the radium of metal cations. The TMOs structures can be measured with a model of close packing of oxygen anions with the development of many categories of interstitial sites such as cubic, octahedral, tetrahedral and triangular sites. Literature study demonstrates that recently controlling morphology and structure of 3D metal oxides is gaining significant attention in materials science, technology and engineering. These materials are extensively used in drug delivery, tissue engineering, catalysis, wastewater treatment, optical devices, batteries and supercapacitors [33]. The effectiveness of 3D metal oxides for different applications depends upon their shape, size, assemblies, crystalline structures and porosity. In this report, different aspects of 3D nanomaterials are described with particular emphasis on their industrial and biological application. To the best of our understanding, this will be the first report on the same topic.

It was suggested some potential innovations and future directions based on the review:
-
Developing new 3D nanomaterial synthesis methods to produce metal oxides and other nanomaterials with precisely controlled size, shape, porosity, and surface chemistry. This could enable tuning of properties for specific applications.
-
Exploring the use of 3D nanomaterials as platforms for multi-functional, smart coatings and surfaces. For example, combining self-healing metal oxides with other responsive nanomaterials to create coatings that can both self-repair and adapt/respond to environmental stimuli.
-
Leveraging 3D morphologies like hollow spheres, mesoporous structures, nanotubes, etc. for advanced applications like drug delivery, catalysis, energy storage. The high surface area and tunable release properties of 3D nanomaterials make them promising for these areas.
-
Investigating new 3D nanomaterial composites by incorporating graphene, carbon nanotubes, or other nanomaterials. This could lead to synergistic effects and enhanced properties compared to the individual components.
-
Expanding the library of 3D nanomaterials beyond metal oxides, to include novel chalcogenides, nitrides, etc. This could uncover unique properties and applications.
-
Studying the environmental and biological impacts of 3D nanomaterials to ensure their safe and sustainable use in commercial products and devices. Developing “greener” synthesis routes.
-
Applying advanced characterization techniques like in-situ TEM, spectroscopy, etc. to gain fundamental insights into 3D nanomaterial growth mechanisms, structure–property relationships, and behavior under applied conditions.

Nanoparticles (NPs) are typically considered colloidal particles ranging from 10 to 100 nm and NPs can be classified as; zero-dimensional (0D), one-dimensional (1D), two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) based on their orientation at various axis[34]. Decreased size-to-charge ratio and increased surface-to-volume ratio allow large surface area, making them useful for various applications. Usually, NPs exhibit magnetic properties that can be applied for different purposes. Recently scientists from related streams focused on the various biomedical applications of different nanomaterials such as carbon allotropes, graphene, fullerene, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, metal NPs and carbon dots [35,36]. Their biomedical applications include drug delivery, cell labelling, gene delivery, hyperthermia, bioimaging and photoablation therapy etc. due to their increased surface-to-volume ratio small size, high chemical stability, high reactivity, toxic-free, magnetic susceptibility, high saturation magnetism, cost-effective, environmental-friendly, biodegradability and biocompatibility [37,38]. Recent review work mainly focused on the current biomedical applications of various nano-system which include carbon allotropes to magnetic metallic compounds and binary and ternary composite [39,40]. Three-dimensional materials such as; liquid metal-based nanoparticles, metal-based nanomaterials, carbon dots as recent generation material, graphene-based quantum dot NPs, carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon-based quantum dots, nanotubes, nano-fibres and graphene are mainly focused in the present study due to their essential characteristics such as flexibility, chemical reactivity, stability and magnetic property etc [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon allotropes-based 3D nanomaterials and quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene nanomaterials can be considered the most fascinating novel class of 3D-Nano materials due to their various superior characteristics such as mechanical, structural, electrical, thermal, optical and many other properties [43]. Graphene is a 2D material itself but when it is fabricated with other nanomaterials such as quantum dots, metal oxides and metal NPs etc. their structural orientation changed into the 3D nanostructure [44]. In the last two decades, these graphene-based nanomaterials attract the attention of scientists and researchers. Graphene nanoparticles (GNP) can be applied in various fields such as photodynamics, therapies, targeted delivery of drug-gene molecules, tissue engineering, bio-imaging and other recent bio applications [45,46] due to its superior properties such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [47], fluorescence properties, mechanical properties, outstanding electrical properties, surface functionality and aqueous processability [48]. Graphene-based nanomaterials such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), reduced graphene oxide-based nanomaterials and fabricated graphene nanostructured material synthesized by sonication of colloidal suspension dispersion method top to bottom or bottom to top approach etc. The size of synthesized GQDs is small and typically ranges from 5 to 20 nm [49]. Usually, GQDs contain some hydrophilic functional groups such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl at the age of those that exhibited properties similar to graphene oxide or reduced graphene oxide. Due to the presence of these groups, it can be further modified with various biological species in organic molecules as per requirement and can be imprinting them which excellent water solubility [50].

Also, eco-friendly GCDs can be fabricated when Quantum dots such as CDs, Pbs and CdSe are treated which graphene [51]. For biomedical applications cytocompatibility of graphene-mediated nanomaterials with microorganisms and living organisms impart an important role which solely depends on the number of sheets, size, structure hydrophobicity and physical properties [52]. Hang et al. demonstrated in their research that hydrophilic forms of graphene can promote cell adhesion i.e. they reported the formation of neutron cells by laminin-coated graphene oxide (GO) without using any biological cues. Similarly in vivo biocompatibility of graphene-mediated nanomaterials has been studied in some recent research. Usually, the biocompatibility of graphene-based nanomaterials also depends on its various physicochemical properties which include surface functionalization, structural orientation size and reactivity [53]. Similarly, Singh and Co-worker reported the effect of reduced graphene oxide rGO and GO on platelets [54].

Graphene-based 3D nanomaterials fabricated with polymers, metals, metal oxides etc. reported as superior biosensors due to their some advantages over normal graphene or reduced graphene oxide. Some of the important biosensor properties such as field effect, transistor, biosensors fluorescence by sensors and electrochemical biosensors have been discussed below:

Due to the sensitivity and specificity of graphene-based 3D materials in the field of electrochemical biosensors for the past two decades, it is being used for sensing various biomolecules. In the case of graphene-based nanomaterials, the reduction and oxidation caused by electron transfer between analyte molecules and graphene nanomaterials is the basis of the electrochemical sensor. It is well known that diabetes become the Global Public Health and to overcome these problems graphene-based 3D nanomaterials are used extensively to detect Glucose levels in diabetic patients. For glucose sensing, oxidizing agent cellulose oxidase enzyme oxidized the glucose to gluconic acid and transfers electrons to oxygen which can be converted to hydrogen peroxide. This molecule can be easily detected using an electrochemical sensor [44]. Similarly, Razmi and coworkers reported GOx-GQDs/carbon/ceramic-based electrochemical biosensors which exhibit efficiency between 5 and 1270 μM concentration of glucose with 1.73 μM detection limit with 0.085 mA cm−2 M−1 sensitivity. Also, an enzymatic detection method based on graphene/gold nanoparticles/chitosan composites electrochemical sensor has also been reported [55]. Additionally, Fig. 3 corresponds to the detection of glucose concentrations ranging from 2 to 16 mM in which calibration curves correspond to amperometric responses at −0.2 and 0.5 V with a scan rate of 0.05 V S-1. Li et al. reported the comparative study of the electrochemical behaviour of enzyme NADH on both reduced graphene oxide film/ Glassy carbon (rGSF/GC) electrode and reduced graphene sheet and they demonstrated that NADH oxidized at 0.4 V and 0.75 V at rGSC/GC electrode and GC the electrode respectively [56].

Fluorescence spectroscopy provides a sensitive, reliable and superior platform for biomolecules detection. Previous studies reported the 3D graphene nanomaterials-based fluorogenic biosensor for the detection of label-free biosensor and glycoprotein and sugar ligand on cancer cells. Ran and co-workers developed a GQDs-based 3D Nanomaterial biosensor for the rapid detection of Ag + and biological thiols which includes glutathione, homocysteine and cysteine selectively. The CQDs-based sensor can detect up to 4.1 nM of glutathione, 4.5 nM homocysteine, 6.2 nM of cystine and 3.5 nM concentration of Ag + ion [57]. Qiu et al. reported the selective detection of glucose by using fabricated boron graphene quantum dot (BGQD) as shown in Fig. 4 a. due to unique aggregation-induced photoluminescence and enhanced the property of boron-based 3D nanomaterials. Also, Fig. 4b demonstrates the possible aggregation-induced photoluminescence increasing mechanism for the selective detection of glucose [58].

LMNPs have many such specific properties of materials that place them in the category of biomaterials, due to which they are used for various biomedical applications on large scale. A mostly metal-based alloy such as 3D nanomaterials and bulk form of the oxide layer is the key feature of LMNPs [59]. The chemical oxidation of LMNPs in an aqueous medium in the presence of external stimulation such as heat exposure and light irradiation causes drastic changes in its morphology. This morphological transformation property of LMNPs makes them a superior, very appropriate candidate for cancer treatment [60]. Metal-based LMNPs specially Ga-mediated LMNPs are a very recent and thrust area for research which having many future opportunities in the field of cancer therapy. For instance, Zhu and co-workers reported the inorganic silica nano cell-based LMNPs towards the multi therapeutic applications as demonstrated in Fig. 5
a [61]. Similar work has also been reported in which LMNPs multifunctional GQD-coated 3D nanomaterials can deliver the DOX and application for cancer therapy by disturbing the interior of cancer cells via shape and size transformation as shown in Fig. 5 b [62].

Similar work towards biomedical applications of LMNPs was studied extensively previously [63–65]. Hou and coworkers fabricated a PTT- cryoablation for the treatment of melanoma by using a Ga- liquid metal-based platform and Ga-In-Cu paste as base biosensor material [65]. In both bulk and liquid form Ga-metal has been used extensively for the generation of clear CT images [66] and better substituents of iohexol which is a traditional x-ray contrast agent [67]. Aversa and coworkers (2016) reported the 3D-nanodiamond fabricated materials for various structural biomedical applications due to their advantages in structural and chemical reactive properties [68]. Additionally, Zhang et al. (2018) summarized the synthesis method chemical and physical properties stability and various applications of 3D-diamond-based nanocomposites [69]. Jalili and coworkers (2013) reported the organic solvent-based Go-liquid crystal toward self-assembled layer-by-layer multifunctional 3D nanostructured material for biomedical applications [70].

Also various carbon allotropes of three-dimensional nanostructure exhibit various important and current applications in the biomedical fields such as cancer therapy, DNA RNA repairing, bio-imaginary and bio-sensing etc. Gyroid graphene and aerogel graphene are the recently developed new forms of graphene with advanced structural orientation size and chemical reactivity. Recent research reported the fabrication of 3D printable graphene by combining polylactide-co-glycolide (3DG) with graphene. The fabricated new 3D nanomaterial added the human mesenchymal stem cell (hMSC) adhesion neurogenic differentiation multiplicity and survival [71]. Also, Jakus and coworkers reported the 3D printing of high-content graphene scaffolds for both biomedical and electrical applications [72]. Carbon nanomaterial-based 3D graphene foam (GF) loaded with mesenchymal stem cells exhibited the wound closer in animal biosample i.e. enhanced damaged skin wound healing [73].

3D nanodiamond materials are used extensively in various biological applications due to their advanced techniques such as low cytotoxicity, functionalization, purification and production techniques [74]. Puzyr and coworkers reported that the activity of enzyme adsorbed on 3D Diamond nanoparticles retained and commercially produced protein has also been purified [75]. This technique is less time-consuming than it takes to a minimum of 30 to 60 min and can be executed easily for this there is no need for costly and sophisticated equipment. Also, these techniques provide error-free data and don't leave impurity [76]. Zhang et al. Reported the hetero-functional nanodiamond mediated drug delivery component which provided the imaging capability in targeting and enhancing therapy. This study mainly focused on the fabrication of hetero-functional nanodiamonds by applying anti-epidermal growth factor receptor and PTX-DNA conjugates onto the 3D nanodiamond surface [77].

3D structured metal-based nanomaterials exhibited excellent antimicrobial mechanisms and were applied to multiple biomedical applications which include drug delivery systems, dentistry tissue culture and engineering implant catheters due to their potential properties such as antimicrobial resistance capacity diminishing efficiency and cytotoxicity. Additionally, metal oxide-based materials especially from early transition metals and first transition series show superior potential properties such as wave density, charging behaviour, ferroelectric, electrochemical, dielectric, electrooptic, catalytic activity etc. [78,79]. These excellent properties enable the selective use of some selective reaction which includes electrocatalysis, photocatalysis, dehydration and oxidation and due to these physicochemical properties MO attract the attention of research and scientist in biomedical application [80]. Some recent research opened a new way of research on MO for various biomedical applications such as cancer treatment [81], biological sensors, retinol therapy [82], cancer cell killer, targeted diagnostics, therapeutics drug delivery and external magnetic fields [83].

Metal oxides can be synthesized in various sizes and shapes by controlled synthesis method and mostly MONPs size ranging from 1 to 100 nm. Due to this compact and smaller size, MONPs have many distinct properties as compared to their bulk or micro-sized counterparts [84]. Due to their smaller size and enhanced surface area MONPs can easily cross the blood–brain barrier and can cross the internal cellular organelles and cell membrane. Metal oxides of transition metal and main group elements play a vital role in biomedical applications. Some important metal oxides with their biomedical applications are discussed as follows:

TiO2 is non-toxic in vivo and vitro as well as less toxic and chemically stable. Due to its properties, it is used in medical implants. Controlled composition and porosity synthesized TiO2 NPs are used for protein adsorption and enhanced tissue attachment of implants. TiO2 also exhibited bone bonding property due to the presence of hydroxyl group and this bonding property can be enhanced by incorporating doping agents such as chlorine magnesium and calcium with titanium [85]. Some previous studies revealed that TiO2 NPs executed anticancer, antifungal and antibacterial properties and when incorporated with curcumin in polymer patches exhibited excellent antibacterial activity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [86,87].

Iron oxide nanoparticles represent the most important class of inorganic nanoparticles in which superparamagnetic iron oxide (SPIONS) used action extensively towards drug molecule delivery imaging and hyperthermia treatment due to their biocompatibility, biodegradable, lees-toxic and easily eluting from the human body. Some research works reported that intrinsic enzyme mimetic activity (oxidizing agent for organic matter) tumor cell treatment and intrinsic therapeutic effect on tumor cell of iron nanoparticles [88,89]. CN Dong et al. (2015) reported the iron oxide nanoparticles as chemotherapeutic drug delivery system which depends on ability of NPs to specific organ in the human body [89]. Iron oxide nanoparticles can be broadly classified as ferromagnetic or super-paramagnetic in which letter in NPs exhibited strong magnetic response to the external applied magnetic field [89].

Additionally, iron oxide nanoparticles exhibit specific properties such as surface engineering potential, colloidal stability, biocompatibility and chemical reactivity proving them as prominent candidates for biomedical applications. Iron oxide nanoparticle is also suitable for Vivo applications due to their ease and degradable property. Hematite (α Fe2O3), maghemite (γ Fe2O3) and magnetite (Fe3O4) are the important class of iron oxide nanoparticles exhibiting magnetic and some other potential behaviour. Increased antimicrobial activity, biocompatibility and stability can be achieved for iron oxide nanoparticles by fabricating/coating them with natural or synthetic polymers such as chitosan, bio-active molecules (bovine serum albumin) inorganic material (silica) and organic surfactant (oleic acid) [90]. Also, hybrid iron oxide nanomaterials exhibited enhanced fictionalization properties and these hybrid materials are copper-iron oxide [91], magnesium-iron oxide[92], gold-iron oxide[93] and silver-iron oxide[94]. Root canal treatment iron oxide exhibit property similar to a peroxide-like activity used as the catalyst for hydrogen peroxide activation [95]. By applying co-precipitation methods or pulsed laser ablation method, magnetic iron oxide and materials of size ranging from 50 to 200 nm were repaired successfully which exhibited bactericidal activity against Serratia marcescens, P. aeruginosa and E. Coli.

Zinc oxide exhibit some superior properties such as non-toxic, cost-effective, eco-friendly, ease of synthesis, high selectivity and biocompatibility and these properties are determined by size and composition. Also, some recent works exhibited antiproliferative activity against lung cancer cells in vitro, cytotoxicity and repairing of the cancerous cells by ZnO nanoparticles [96–98]. Ngoepe and coworkers reported the antimicrobial property of ZnO against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. Also, FDA listed it safe inorganic material so it is used as a food beverage preservative and also shows antimicrobial properties [99]. Ananth et al. (2018) exhibit the bone repair and bone regeneration of ZnO NPs in their study. Also fabricated composite of Zn with magnesium substituted hydroxyapatite (MnHAp) and its bilayer coating with type 316L stainless steel provided a suitable alternative for orthopaedic applications [100].

Silver oxide nanoparticle is the most used inorganic materials for earth antiviral, antifungal and antimicrobial activity [101]. Moreover, silver oxide nanoparticles are also applied as antimicrobial moieties in various industries such as textile manufacturing [102], drinking water, disinfection [103] and food packaging [104]etc. Additionally, antimicrobial scaffolds developed for both hard and soft tissue engineering can be applied mainly for wound dressing, and bone regeneration [105,106]. Glass substituted Ag2O nanoparticles and silver doped hydroxyapatite has been employed for bone regeneration as filler in bone [107] and for dental and orthopaedic applications as an antimicrobial coating [108]. The fabricated hybrid nanocomposite of Ag2O (5 nm size) loaded with TiO2 nanotube via photoreduction method at a fixed temperature (Fig. 6 ) [109]. This composite material exhibited superior antimicrobial activity against S. aureus which is further enhanced to greater than 99.9% after one-hour irradiation with sunlight [109]. Additionally, Fig. 6 demonstrated the photocatalytic activity of Ag2O with TiO2 nanotube responsible for antimicrobial behaviour a) photocatalytic mechanism under ultraviolet light and b) photocatalytic activity involved under visible light.

Copper oxide-based nanoparticles exhibited some unique properties as compared to silver or gold oxide nanoparticle which includes cheaper, green nature, ease of availability biodegradability and potential behaviour towards microbes and fungus. CuO nanoparticles can be synthesized from both chemical substances and the green synthetic route and so CuO NPs synthesized from the green route exhibited lower toxicity as compared to the chemical route can make them a suitable candidate for biomedical applications. CuO NPs synthesized from extracts of different parts of plants exhibit potential antimicrobial activity against persistent bacteria such as P. aeruginosa, E-coli, S. aureus and B. Subtilis [110]. Similarly, CuO NPs prepared from other green routes exhibited anticancer activity and antimicrobial activity [111].

Some of the metal oxides such as aluminium oxide, cerium oxide and magnesium oxide are also important classes of metal oxides apart from the well-known stabilized metal oxide discussed in the previous section. For instance, Ce(IV) based cerium oxide nanoparticle (CeO2) exhibited good redox active potential behaviour due to inverting capability of oxidation state between + 3 and + 4. Due to this converting capability, it can be applied as potential material for anticancer, antioxidants and antibacterials [112,113]. The cerium oxide nanoparticles also exhibited activity against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria via indirect and direct interaction [114]. Similarly, pure magnesium is seldom applied as an antimicrobial agent [115] but hybrid material of magnesium is used extensively in combating infections. Interaction of magnesium oxide in a material with bacteria causes induced oxidative stress membrane leakage and ultimately cell death [116]. Also biocompatibility and antibacterial property exhibited by composite material in which titanium surface is fabricated with a very thin and repeat coding sheet of magnesium oxide nanoparticles [117].

In the native form, aluminium oxide is applied extensively for some potential applications such as alloy formation, drug delivery systems, personal care products, biosensor energetic, wear resistance coating for ship products, fuels and explosives etc. At a wide concentration range i.e. 10–1000 ug mL−1 of aluminium oxide nanoparticles showed inhibitory activity towards E-coli bacteria [118]. When aluminium oxide (Al2O3) nanomaterial is fabricated with other potential organic or inorganic substances its activity as an antimicrobial barrier has increased. For instance, aroma water retention and oxygen concentration can be improved when aluminium oxide surface is coated with organic substances such as alginate chitosan layer, double coating polylactic acid or polylactic acid-aluminium oxide and a composite which is widely applied as antimicrobial material for edible items packing. Excellent antimicrobial properties are exhibited by fabricated material composed of aluminium oxide nanoparticles and silica titration nanocomposite. So overall it can be concluded that almost all kinds of metal oxide in native form or in the form of composite material exhibit potential biomedical applications.

SPIONs are iron oxide nanoparticles that are used to kill cancer cells. When SPIONs are induced in the presence of an external magnetic field, they kill cancer cells through temperature-induced apoptosis therapy by heating the local cancer area and releasing the loaded drug. Similarly, titanium dioxide nanoparticle is used to kill cancer cells, which is proven by previous studies [119]. TiO2 NPs have the property that they can interact with the cytoplasm and cell membrane of cancerous cells and when exposed to ultraviolet light, they generate ROS which induces apoptosis [119]. Mitjans, Vinaedella and Shrestha et al. reported that magnetic composite material is obtained when SPIONs are fabricated with titanium oxide and act toward cancer cell treatment. Also, TiO2 NPs can remain in the human body for long period without any adverse or side effects [119,120]. Also, cerium oxide nanoparticles exhibited excellent cancer cell treatment from ROS-induced damage due to invisibility in oxidation states between + 4 and + 3. CeO2 NPs also exhibited radio sensitizing property due to which it can control the response to DNA damage.

The main function of the drug delivery system is to deliver the drug molecule to the targeted or affected organ. Metal oxides such as SPIONs, ZnO and CeO2 are commonly used as carriers to deliver certain selected drugs such as gallic acid, rhodium citrate, quercetin, tamoxifen and doxorubicin (DOX). In the medicine field, theragnostic were introduced recently which is applicable when combining selected target therapy with Diagnostic tests. Some of the current research reports that drug efficiency and delivery can be easily monitored by combining drug delivery systems with contrast materials used to visualize cancer cells. Research revealed that ZnO NPs are seldom used materials, which can perform diagnostics and therapeutic functions. Similarly, FDA proved that SPIONs are a contrast agent for MRI imaging systems by using an external magnet when combined as SPIONs drug conjugate. This strategy is applied as a potential technique to kill the cancer sale efficiently without any harm. The study proved that DOX-loaded SPIONs work together efficiently to kill breast cancer and this approach lowers the side effects and reduced the doses of drug molecules [121].

In the previous section antibacterial activity, implants and wound healing treatment are based on metal oxide nanoparticles in which copper oxide, titanium oxide, ferrous oxide, ferric oxide and silver oxide play a vital role in this. Azam and co-workers reported the activity towards bactericidal infection and efficient reactivity towards Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [122]. Also, they reported the antimicrobial activity of ferrous oxide, copper oxide and zinc oxide nanoparticles against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria [122]. Similarly, bone healing treatment is another thrust area of research which is mainly focused on the development of drug molecules responsible for the fast healing of wounds. In this regard, zinc oxide nanoparticles are used frequently for wound healing [123]. Also, Titanium nanoparticles proved an efficient wound healing agent when irritated with UV light. TiO2 NPs can responsible for the formation of ROS (OH radical), and superoxide anion which can oxidize the cell membrane and lipids of the bacteria could reduce DNA damage [124].

Electricity is mainly a significant energy source in the modern era. Wearable technology, emerging internet of things, electrical grids, electrical devices, medical equipment, lab instruments, and portable consumer electronics technology rests primarily on reversible energy storage and electricity [125]. Importance of energy storage harvested by solar panels, moving machinery, artificial intelligence, heat source power generators wind power, geothermal power etc. [126]. There is still a need for considerable improvement and diversification of various energy storage techniques. Energy conversion technologies such as supercapacitors, capacitors, batteries, solar cells and clear fuel production etc.[127]. can be improved if the electrochemical process of material can be understood as both are closely connected to each other in order to understand the fundamental concept of energy conversion, storage and transformation. Due to some unusual electric, optical and mechanical properties of 3D nanomaterials and metal oxides, the researcher's attention was drawn to the materials. Usually, nanomaterials and metal oxides behave like electrocatalysts, by which they lower the energy barrier of the electrochemical process as well as promote electron charge transfer over the surface of the material [128].

Usually, 3D nanomaterials are composed of variously formed nano-objects such as nano specified geometric, sheets, wires, rods, dots, spherical sizes and shapes. Based on the above confinement of dimension; there are mainly four forms of nanomaterials that are matters of interest which includes zero, one, two and three dimensional. In this category, 3D nanomaterials are those materials which exist in their dimension i.e. X Y and Z axis. 3D nano-particles such as fullerene, carbon dots, carbon nanotubes, graphene, metallic materials etc. are important classes of 3D Nanomaterials. Thematical, electrical behaviour and chemical reactivity are some key characteristics of three-dimensional nanoparticles [129,130].

Similarly, metal oxides are considered important materials from the point of view of scientific and technological approaches. Various metallic oxides are being used industrially as metal oxide thin films for the past several decades. In general, metal oxides can be amorphous or crystalline in nature and show various optical and electrical structural properties. By modifying these properties easily, the electrical property of metal oxide can be changed into a new form, which can also be applied by depositing it in the new material [131,132]. Therefore controlling the film properties of metal oxide in a derived way is an important parameter by which the desired film preparation can be done in various advanced applications such as solar cells, fuel cells, catalysis sensors, piezoelectric cells, microelectronics circuits, electrode material for supercapacitors [133].
There are severe advanced techniques to synthesize the 3D nanomaterials those are discussed here:

Electrodeposition is a very useful technique for the production of sulfides, hydroxides and d orbital metal oxides and hierarchical structures. In addition, electron position is an advanced technique in many ways compared to the hydrothermal method, among which short deposition time, excellent safety, low material waste, accuracy, and low processing temperature are prominent. The research on metal sulfides electrodeposited exhibited some advanced findings and gives an idea to replace costly platinum. The energy-efficient and cost-effective electrode has been introduced with a hierarchically integrated 3D array structure which is composed of CoS2 sheet, carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and carbon paper and exhibits stability and high activity [134]. In addition to similar metal chalcogenides, electrodeposition of 3D transition metal hydroxides has also been achieved in the presence of 3D metal cation and nitrate anion electrolyte [135,136].

The use of MOFs for electrocatalytic applications is nowadays used as a precursor for the synthesis of useful porous metallic-based nanomaterials. Generally, this technique is very useful due to flexibility in nanofabrication, structure modulation under different conditions and good tunability of MOFs synthesis. Research reported that hybrid highly porous nanowire (NW) arrays composed of carbon and Co3O4 can be synthesized by using facile carbonization of a metal–organic framework and copper foil [137].

The hydrothermal synthesis method is a very powerful technique for the preparation of transition metal compounds such as sulfides, hydroxides, and oxides to functional nanomaterials. Many researchers reported that at hydrothermal synthesis method self-organized hierarchical Nano structures such as Nano urchins and Nano flowers can be obtained.

Above mentioned materials can be applied as precursors or catalysts for application in OER and HER. For instance, the RuO2 catalysts' OER performance can be comparable with the hydrothermally prepared 3D hierarchical nanostructure Alpha [Ni(OH)2] catalysts [138]. The use of nanoscale substrate templates for nano structure design provides a reliable path for the growth of nanomaterials. For instance, 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are being used to prepare a variety of 3D hybrid nanomaterials and the synthesized nanomaterial exhibited excellent chemical stability, good electron conductivity and large surface area etc. [139,140]. CNF-mediated 3D (three-dimensional) nanomaterials are increasing in the fields of environmental science, biomedical, engineering, catalysis, sensors, separation, adsorption, energy generation and storage. Commonly, CNF-based 3D nanomaterials are produced using different strategies such as hydrothermal synthesis, template synthesis, chemical vapour deposition, electrospinning and other assorted techniques. The energy sector of 3D CNF-based nanomaterials such as supercapacitors, electrochemical batteries, fuel cells, solar cells etc. High mechanical strength, lightweight, corrosion resistance, mutually porous structure, and the large surface area of ​​CNF-based 3D nanomaterials [141,142].

The electro-spinning method of 3D CNFs by structuring modulated nanofibers spanning polymer processes is an advanced and effective tool. The high voltage of the special collector and the spinning nozzle was applied for processing this method. In the process of spraying the polymer is refined and stretched to continue the nanofibers. Finally, the completion of the required step is followed by pre-treatment such as calcination or carbonization to essentially form 3D carbon nanofibers [143,144]. Furthermore, electrospinning is an advanced and easy technique to fabricate 3D PNF-based scaffolds [145]. Also, electro-spinning 2D members and post-treatment of 1D PNF is another advanced technique to fabricate 3D PNF structure behind direct electro-spinning fabrication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Combined strategies for synthesis of CNF- based 3D nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: In this method, two or more two techniques combined were applied for the preparation of 3D CNF-based nano-structured materials. For intense researchers reported the combined techniques i.e. CVD and electro-spinning in order to fabricate the CNF nanostructured material 3D hybrid Ni multiwall carbon nanotube (Ni-MWCNFs) [146]. In this regard, Fig. 7 reveals the production of 3D (Ni-MWCNFs) by using both CVD electrospinning and carbonization in which Fig. 7a demonstrated the synthesis processor, solution preparation and electrospinning with SEM microphotographs of as-spun mart. Fig. 7b and 7c exhibited the CVD unit and expected surface morphological image of electrospinning CNFs, composites Ni/CNFs and hybrid 3D Ni/MWCNFs.

Supercapacitors are highly advanced energy storage devices that are often used as a suitable alternative to various batteries and capacitors. Some of the distinctive properties of supercapacitors such as long cycle life, fast charging, large electrical capacity etc. make them superior energy storage devices. Recently, nanostructured materials are specialized 3D nanomaterials composed of carbon allotropes, polymers, metal oxides, and binary and ternary composites. For instance, CNFs-based 3D nanomaterials are widely used for the fabrication of supercapacitors due to their specific chemical and physical property which include cost-effectiveness, greenness stability at high temperatures excellent conductivity, high surface area etc. Some previous researchers demonstrated that CNFs-based 3D nanomaterials applied extensively towards supercapacitor fabrication by achieving good cycle stability, high-rate capacity and high ratio capacitance. Studied revealed the enhanced high specific capacitance, good cycle stability and superior rate capability of 3D-CNFs nanomaterials by doping suitable hetero atoms (B, N, S) to the nanostructured and surface modification [147,148].

Similarly, carbon aerogels have the characteristics of cost-effective, suitable and stable mechanical properties, a 3D interconnecting CNF network structure, excellent connectivity and low density. The study suggested the tiny holes on their 3D nanostructure can allow the diffusion and penetration of ions and lead to further uses for energy storage and convention [149].

Similarly, carbon metal oxide composites such as MnO2/ CNS demonstrated superior stability with capacitance retention of 96.1% at a current density of % 5Ag-1 after 5000 cycles [150]. Moreover, hybrid nano-materials such as Co3O4/ graphene [151] and Co3O4/ CNFs [152] were synthesized and resulted in a large surface area in order to improve electrical conductivity. Carbons-CPs and metal oxide CPs-based composites were also used as a supercapacitor n the same previous reported studies. Yang et al. synthesized rGO aerogel-PANI composite by applying electro-decomposition techniques in which PANI arrays were deposited on the aerogel. The fabricated composite material exhibited a specific capacitance of 432 Fg−1 at the current density at 1 Ag−1. Also provided an energy density of 25 WhKg−1 and more than 80% of capacitance remained after 1000 cycles and composites materials with various bending mode is highly stable at high temperature [153]. Moreover enhanced electrochemical performance in supercapacitors is achieved by combining MOS-CPs as alternate composite materials. Composites materials MOS-CPs demonstrated enhanced cycle stability, rate capacity, and specific capacitance as compared to individual MOS-CPs [154,155].

A fuel cell is a converter that converts chemical energy into electrical energy efficiently. It is cost-effective, less time-consuming and environmentally friendly. In fuel cell mainly electrochemical reaction takes place, in which two separate semiconductors i.e. the reduction of the cathode and oxidation of the anode occurs. Usually, the fuel cell consists of platinum-based electrocatalysis which is very costly and has low reserves. To overcome these problems cost-effective, easily available and stable nanomaterials attract the attention of researchers in the field. In this regards CNFs based 3D nanomaterials can be suitably substituted for the above Pt-based electrocatalysts. Some important characteristics of CNFs based 3D materials such as mediating electron transfer [156], stronger electron chemical reactivity, availability, of more active sites, improve service life [157,158] makes low price, high redox activity, high potential and stable performance then better alternative to the Pt-based electro-catalysts. The fabricated 3D CNFs-based nanostructured electrode materials show superior performance for bio-electrochemical systems such as microbial electrolysis cells and microbial fuel cells. In this regard Khare et al. reported the fabrication of CNF skinned three-dimensional Ni/carbon micropillars with an advanced lased-ablation technique. This study suggested the large surface area, high biocompatibility, and excellent electrical conductivity with promising potential for microbial fuels [159].

Also, Shi et al. revealed the self-designed multi-needle electrospinning setup synthesis of N/B doped microporous 3D CNF-based materials, which show superior metal-free catalysis towards the oxygen reduction reaction in the collective media. These 3D CNF nanostructured materials can be successfully applied to fuel cells [160].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical hydrogen evolution from water splitting
ce:section.ce:para: Pollution-free and limited fossil fuel, solar energy, and the resources trying hard can be easily replaced with clean and renewable energy like hydrogen in the near future and research work is in progress in this area. Currently, the electrolysis of water is used frequently for hydrogen evaluation. In this context, various functional materials have been used for the electrochemical hydrogen evolution of food as a catalyst to enhance the decomposition of water and energy utilization [161,162].

Yang and co-workers reported that Pt-Co alloy nanoparticles encapsulated CNFs exhibited superior performance in electrochemical hydrogen generation [161]. Similarly, WOs-based nanoplates fabricated on CNFS are used as electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reactions. Also, some characteristics of CNFS-based 3D nanomaterials such as high chemical stability, wide surface area, multi-active sites and inter-connected structures proved them promising materials for the production of hydrogen through electrocatalyst hydrogen evolution reaction [HER] [163,164]. Gu et al. fabricated and synthesised advanced 3D nanoelectrocatalyst CNF-based material to produce hydrogen from high-performance HER. In addition, some transition metal NPs and noble materials were fabricated with 3D CNF, and 3D nanomaterials to synthesise hybrid 3D nanomaterials to enhance the electrocatalytic performance [165,166].

It was found the key points from the electrochemical water splitting discussion:
-
Heterostructures like zinc iron oxide (ZnFe2O4) and polyoxometalate (POM) nanoplates can improve electrocatalytic activity for HER and O

ER due to high surface area, electronic/chemical modulation, and heterojunction formation.
-
Composites like nickel–iron sulfide (NiFeS) nanosheets on MXene-coated nickel foam can optimize HER/OER adsorption energies and achieve high alkaline electrolyzer efficiency.
-
Mixed transition metal oxides like Mn3O4, Co3O4 with CuO, NiO show synergistic improvements in HER/OER activities and kinetics compared to individual components.
-
Low overpotentials, reduced impedance, and structural properties like high surface area and electron transport contribute to enhanced composite electrocatalyst performance.
-
Integrating efficient composite catalysts with photovoltaics enables practical solar-driven water splitting for H2 production at large scales and reduced costs.

The key focus is on how composite catalysts with multiple transition metal oxides/chalcogenides/MXenes can achieve synergistic improvements in alkaline water electrolysis performance for efficient and economical hydrogen production [167–169].

CNF material 3D nanomaterials also used electrode materials for electrochemical batteries such as K-ion batteries (KIBS) Na ion batteries (NIBs) and Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are extensively applied as a power source for mobile phones, sophisticated toys e-vehicles, notebooks and some other electronic devices. Some previous researchers demonstrated that CNF-based 3D materials are used as precursor materials for the preparation of cathode in lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) due to their superior characteristics such as porous structure, high recyclability, good capacitance, high specific surface area, excellent conductive [170,171]. High effort is currently being put into enhancing the performance of LIBs to develop their better cycle stability, high power density, higher energy density and electrochemical performance. The use of CNFs-based 3D nanomaterials matrix as substrate materials is the best way to solve lithium metal dissolution/deposition problems. Using the annealing process Zhang et al. prepared electrode materials by fabricating ultra-fine oxides N-enriched 3D CNFs to solve conductive CNF metrics and active materials [172].

On the other hand, Na+ is abundant and easy availability in cation from Li+ better substituent previous published work suggested that CNFs and CNFs doped with Sn, S, N, and other elements have been synthesised and utilised for other fabrication of NIBs [173–175]. In the same manner, Wang et.al. reported the synthesis of 3D CNF nanostructured thin film co-doped with N and B (BN-CNFs) to achieve superior rate capacity and high specific capacity[176].

Also KIBS can be regarded as the best alternative to both LIBS and NIBS due to higher electrode potential, higher energy density, and stronger working voltage of K+ as compared to Li+ and Na+. Previous studies reported the 3D CNFs nanomaterials fabricated with SnO2, MoO2, silica NPs and W to improve the electrochemical behaviour of electrode materials [177–179]. In addition, 3D group inverse nanomaterial is also used extremely extensively in electrochemical energy storage devices. Usually, 3D graphene-based nanomaterials exhibited some useful characteristics which it can utilize in different energy fields such as sensing, energy and various environmental aspects. 3D IC-rGO nanomaterials exhibited higher capacitance and superior stability and their performance increased [180].

From a scientific and technological point of view, metal oxides can be considered the most important class of material and due to their key characteristics, it is being used in various filed. Metal oxides have all the properties like energy storage and capacitance, which have challenged the primacy of superconductors like silicon [181]. The physicochemical properties of metal oxides also depend on their method of preparation and operating condition. Moreover, the optical, electrical and structural properties of the film can be modified by changing the deposition process and condition. So by controlling the deposit film properties of metal oxides, its applications can be fabricated in the form of sensors, piezoelectric, catalysis, fuel cells, and microelectronic circuits. Some important metal advocates are metal oxide such as cadmium oxide (CdO), cuprous oxide Cu2O, tin oxide (SnO2), tungsten oxide (WO3), titanium oxide (TiO2), zinc oxide (ZnO) recently applied towards electrochemical energy application. Some other metallic oxides such as cupric oxide (Cu2O), molybdenum oxide (MoO2) and vanadium oxide (V2O5) show fascinating multi-functional properties comprising optoelectronics, electronics, and semiconductivity, optical and magnetic etc. [182]. Additionally, metal oxide-based nanomaterials have been considered the most emerging nanostructured materials because of their applications in catalysis, piezoelectricity, biosensors and optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal oxides as electrode materials for supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Transition metal oxides (TMOs) are the most commonly and frequently used redox active nanomaterials used as electrodes for supercapacitors. V, Mo, Ti, Co, Fe, Ni and Cu are some important transition metals incorporated in the TMOs and used recently for different applications. Some metallic oxides such as RuO2, Co3O4, Fe3O4, MnO2, V2O5, CuO, and NiO prefer superior electrode materials due to their higher specific capacitance, high power density and high energy and their charge storage mechanism are based on the faradic behaviours. Moreover in order to enhance the capacitance power of electrode material and energy storage of devices radius mixed MO, Such as Fe2O3, / NiCO2O4 [183], Cu/CuO2, TiO2 [184], Co3O4 [185], ZnO/Co3O4 [186] and CuO/NiO [187] used previously. The electrochemical properties of some transition metal oxides are discussed in brief here.

It was indicate 3D metal oxide as electrode materials for supercapacitor applications [188–190]:
-
Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) were synthesized using Datura metel leaf extract and showed good antimicrobial and antifungal properties. As an electrode material, the Ag NPs exhibited a specific capacitance of 825F/g.
-
A sodium alginate biopolymer film incorporated with Withania somnifera leaf extract (WLISA) was prepared. It showed ionic conductivity of 2.80 × 10–––6 S/cm and an electrochemical window of −0.4 to 0.4 V, making it suitable as an electrolyte for supercapacitors.
-
Ag/NiO nanocomposite was synthesized using Withania somnifera leaf extract via green sol–gel method. The nanocomposite with grain size of ∼ 70 nm showed enhanced electrochemical properties. As an electrode material, it exhibited specific capacitance of 293F/g.
-
The use of plant extracts to synthesize metal oxide nanoparticles, and their incorporation into biopolymer electrolytes/electrodes resulted in materials with good electrochemical performance for supercapacitor applications. The green synthesis methods and biopolymer-based composites make these materials sustainable and eco-friendly.

In summary, the abstracts highlight the potential of sustainable metal oxide nanocomposites synthesized using plant extracts as electrode/electrolyte materials for supercapacitors with appreciable capacitance and electrochemical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transition metal oxide as electrode material for SC
ce:section.ce:para: Vanadium pentaoxide exhibited electrochemical properties which include good stability, specific capacitance, and high rate capability which makes them energy emerging electrode materials. Recent studies demonstrated the synthesis of the V2O5 sheet with activated carbon [191]. Which show specific capacitance of 466 Fg−1, specific energy of 10.7 WhKg−1, specific power of 156 WKg-1and capacitance retention of 77% after 1000 cycles. Similar works were also reported by Xing et. al. in which V2O5 and coralline-shaped carbon based asymmetric supercapacitors [192]. Ruthenium oxide (RuO2) has also some noticeable advantages such as a long lifecycle, high reversible redox reaction, high thermal stability, high conductivity and superior electrochemical performance. Sridhar and co-workers synthesized electricity from CNFs and pseudo-capacities RuO2 at concentrated alkaline electrolytes. The resultant nano capacitors exhibited excellent capacitance of 830 mF/cm2 and out outstanding capacitances retention of 9.4 % even after 5,000 cycles [193]. Similarly, Sharma and co-workers used binder-free cobalt oxide (Co3O4) pseudo capacitive materials to fabricate a symmetric Se. The fabricated metal oxide nanomaterials exhibited specific capacitance of 95 Fg-1and energy density of 29 Wh Kg−1 with superior capacitance retention of 91.4% after10000 cycles were achieved [194]. In addition, research adopted Cu2O as electrode material due to its high conductivity, abundance, cost-effectiveness and superior electrochemical performance. The study reveals the synthesis of cubic copper oxide framework by slow thermal method at 2 M aqueous KOH electrolyte medium. specification such as 365 Fg−1, energy density of 16.95 Wh Kg-1at a power density of 234.4 Wh Kg−1 [195].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mos composites with other materials as electrode materials for SCs
ce:section.ce:para: Besides the individual electrochemical performance of metal oxides, their efficiency can be increased by fabricating within with other materials such as Noble metal oxide, and carbon allotropes in the form of binary and ternary composites. For instanceMnO2/CNT is fabricated and prepared by a hydrothermal process. The synthesis nanocomposite material electrode show superior performance as compared to CNF and pure MnO2 with a specific capacitance of 223 Fg−1 [196]. Gao and co-workers fabricated a hybrid device composed on MnO2/Au/MnO2 nano spikes electrode synthesised from low voltage anodization method, exhibited specific energy of 1.75x 10-3 Wh cm−3 capacitance [197]. Xu.et.al. Fabricated Au-AgO mediated nonporous nanostructured composites material of 10 nm size from electrochemical partial dealloying stripping method, exhibited relatively higher cycle rotation [198]. An and Co-worker prepared Ru-RuO2 nanocomposites (Nb-doped TiO2 nanofibers) using impregnation and electrospinning method with a high rate capacitance of 5 mVs−1 with a specific capacitance of 476 Fg−1 [199]. Ma et al. synthesized ternary Ag/MnO2/rGO composites from two-step in which transition metal and noble metal were deposited on the rGO surface. synthesized fabricated nanocomposites revealed capacitance of 4675 Fg−1 at 5mVs−1 SR. Similarly, the same reactant ternary nanocomposite such as noble metal, carbon allotropes, polymers and metal oxide has been reported extensively with improved electrochemical performance as supercapacitors electrode materials [200–203].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal oxides used in ion batteries
ce:section.ce:para: Various types of metal oxides have been used in some previous studies for the anodes and cathodes of rechargeable ion batteries used in electrochemical energy storage devices [204–207]. Li-ion, Na -ion and divalent ion batteries used are well studied ion batteries used for commercialization purpose. In addition to the anode and cathode in Li-ion batteries generally, lithium-containing electrolyte gel is present in the form of electrolyte solid or solution [208]. Generally, the cathode material in Li-ion batteries is lithiated metal oxides and the anode is typically graphite. However, some current researchers have also used metal oxide as an anode [209]. The reversibility of the above redox process mainly depends on the chemical properties and structure of the metal oxide cathode. usually, oxides such as LiMnO2, LiNiO2, and LiCoO2 having a layered structure in which Li-ions can diffuse at the inner and outer layer of host oxides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal oxides as catalysis for photocatalysis and electrocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: A wide range of electrochemical methods was used to study chemical reaction related to photocatalysis and electrocatalysis energy. The specific impact of metal oxides interface on chemical reactivity has been studied extensively from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Similarly, linear sweep voltammetry or cyclic voltammetry has also been used to study the catalysis of metal oxides. Various types of reactions such as oxygen evolution reaction (OER), the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and various form of carbon dioxide reduction has been investigated mainly with the help of EIS techniques. those are useful for energy applications because they are responsible for the conversion of solar electrical energy into chemical energy etc.
The use of 3D metal oxides as catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution is a promising area of research, with potential implications for sustainable energy production [210–212].

. It is indicated the fabrication of mixed metal oxide composites CuO/NiO/ZrO2 (CNZr) via a hydrothermal route. The CNZr600 composite showed a higher hydrogen generation rate compared to other composites, attributed to its structural properties, high electroactive surface areas, fast electron transport kinetics, and good chemo-electrical bonding between CuO, NiO, and ZrO2. This suggests that the design of mixed metal oxide composite catalysts could be a viable approach for low-cost hydrogen production. The next work introduces a 2D MAX structure comprising of Ti3AlC2 multilayers and a CuO/NiO composite. The composite showed a higher hydrogen generation rate than the CN nanocomposite, which is attributed to the presence of Ti3AlC2 multilayers on CuO/NiO, facilitating excellent photoinduced charge carrier separation via the s-scheme mechanism. This indicates that Ti3AlC2 MAX-based structured catalysts could have potential industrial applications for hydrogen evolution and other ecological energy systems. It was also presents the development of titanium dioxide nanorods sensitized by nickel sulfide, cadmium sulfide, and zinc sulfide nanoparticles (ZnS/CdS/NiS@TiO2) as a photoanode material. The sensitized TiO2 nanorod arrays showed a significantly higher photoelectrochemical hydrogen production compared to pristine TiO2, suggesting the potential of novel hybrids of TiO2 nanorods decorated with transition metal chalcogenides for efficient photoelectrochemical hydrogen production. In conclusion, these studies demonstrate the potential of 3D metal oxides as catalysts for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, offering promising pathways for the development of efficient, cost-effective, and sustainable hydrogen production technologies. However, further research is needed to optimize these materials and processes for practical applications.

Study stead state photo excitation with EIS electrochemical techniques is given to prove the chemical activity of semiconductor metal oxide for OER photocatalysis. The previous studies mainly focuses on BiVO4 [213–215] and Fe2O3 [216,217] due to their visible light absorption and nano band rays. Similarly copper data for sites [218,219] IrO2 [220,221] and ZnO [222,223] with UV photocatalysis are deposited by the recent studies. various researcher reported the OER photocatalysis behavior of BiO4 and Fe2O3 [224,225].

Jeony and co-workers reported the hexavalent dopants (Mo6+, W6+, Cr6+) BiO4 modification of electrolyte, deposition of Co-Pi as a co-catalyst and hetero-junction formation with WO3. The fabricated materials exhibited lower charge transfer resistance for WO3,/BiO3 OER and decreased r ct when it was deposited with W6+ and Mo6+ [226].

Usually, the main objective of electrocatalysis analysis with metal oxides is to explore the specific role of surface termination on catalysis. EIS is a prominent and advanced tool for studying the surface termination and influence of ORR electrocatalysts [227–229] ORR electrocatalysis [230,231]. EIS-based analysis on various metal oxides reported the various metal oxide such as nanotubes nanorods/ nanowires [232], nanosheets [233], and nanoparticles [221].
