For intense Co3O4, nanocube terminated in (100) facets compared to Co3O4 nanorods terminated in (110) surfaces exhibited better OER electrocatalysis (i.e. lower Rct). Also, noble metal oxides termination has also been reported in previous studies [234,235]. Studies demonstrated that their previous metal oxide revealed superior catalysis for ORR as compared to solid oxide fuel cells. Similarly, Lee et. al. reported La2NiO4.8 thin film fabricated for ORR on the (100) facet grown by layer deposition (pulse) on YSZ substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Noble metal, metal oxides, nanostructured composites
ce:section.ce:para: To attract metal oxides such as CeO2, Cu2O, TiO2 and ZnO composites of noble metals such as Pd, Pt, Ag, Au and Au-Ag alloys for photocatalysis, drug delivery, solar cells, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy and other energy conservation-related applications the attention of scientists in the recent time. Metal oxides coupled with Nobel metallic materials to fabricate various diversified nanostructures such Janus metal–metal oxide nanostructures, noble metal oxide yolk/shall, noble metal/ metal oxide core cell, nanoarrays, noble metal decorated metal oxide nanoparticles (NPs) applied extensively for various applications.

Table 1 compares materials used in electrochemical water splitting, photocatalytic hydrogen evolution, and supercapacitor performance. In water splitting, ZnFe2O4-POM shows higher current density and stability but higher overpotential than NiFe LDH. In hydrogen evolution, Ti3AlC2/CuO-NiO has a higher band gap, hydrogen evolution rate, and stability than CuO/NiO/ZrO2. In supercapacitors, NiCo2O4 nanosheets have higher capacitance, energy, and power density but lower cycle stability than nO2 nanowires. These comparisons can guide material selection and development.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing and biosensing application of 3D NPs

ce:section.ce:para: In the development era, the chemical, petroleum, oil recycling, metallurgic and automobile industries play the main role in the ecological problems. This is due to these industrial sections releasing more toxic gas into the atmosphere. The aquatic, soil and atmospheric systems are contaminated with toxic gas. For example, sulfur dioxide (SO2), carbon monoxide (CO), nitrogen oxides (NOx), and various volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are produced from the activities of the above industrial sections. As a result, various disasters occurred, such as diseases (e.g., dyspnea, nerve injury, etc.) and environmental hazards (e.g., smog, tropospheric photochemical pollution, etc.). The various volatile organic compounds (VOCs) contained acetone (CH3OCH3), ethanol (C2H5OH), formaldehyde (HCHO), toluene (C6H5CH3), benzene (C6H6), etc. These compounds are released from various materials such as building materials, life supplies, outdoor industrial waste gases and indoor decoration materials. Therefore, detecting harmful and toxic gas is crucial important [236].

Developing gas sensing materials are an important part of the material and engineering sciences. In modern times, the 3D nanomaterials-based 3D transition metal oxides are interestingly suggested for gas sensing. The reason is their multifunctional properties for gas detection. Table 2 shows the basics of 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides for gas sensing. In general, it is clearly evident that the 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides are super sensitive materials for gas sensing. They also have long-term stability, excellent selectivity, fast response–recovery speed (1–20 s), low detection limit (at ppb level), high sensitivity, recyclable, high selectivity and sensitive at medium and high temperatures. For example, 3D Fe2O3/Fe-honeycomb-like N-doped graphitic carbon nanomaterial (3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC) was prepared and it's gas sensing properties were studded for NO2 [237]. The obtained results confirmed that the low values of NO2 gas were accurately detected by the 3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC at room temperatures. The selectivity and sensitivity of this material for gas sensing are also very high. The sensitivity concentrations of NO2 gas on the 3D Fe2O3/Fe@N-GC detection was 25.48–100 ppm at 2.13 s. The sensitive concentration limit is 10 ppb. The recycling time is 11.73 s and stability is 2 months. In other research work [238], the flower-like 3D ZnO nanostructures were synthesized and their sensing properties for ethanol were studied. As observed, it was found that the ethanol sensing of flower-like 3D ZnO nanostructures was checked at 500 °C. In this sensing, the formation of active ethoxy and oxygen promotes ethanol detection. In the atmosphere, the 3D ZnO nanostructure reacted with the N2 to form the ZnO–N2, which is also responsible for ethanol detection.

Toluene, xylene, acetone, ethyl-acetate and ethanol gases sensing performance of 3D flower-like CuO nanostructures were investigated [239], confirming that the 3D flower-like CuO is excellent sensors for toluene, xylene, acetone, ethyl-acetate and ethanol gases at 1000 ppm. Rg/Ra = 2.8 for toluene; Rg/Ra = 4 for ethanol; Rg/Ra = 4.6 for ethyl-acetate. This material is recyclable, easily recovered and fast response in gas sensing. The gas sensing of 3D hierarchical SnO2 nanostructures for n-butanol was checked [240]. It is noted from the results that the 3D hierarchical SnO2 is superior selectivity, fast response–recovery characteristics, and good reproducibility adding a high response for n-butanol gas at ppm degrees. Its porous structure promotes gas sensing.

Detecting biological objects at low concentrations is crucially important in medicine, pharmacology and biology-related applications. Currently, the 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides are importantly used in biosensing, because, they are superior selectivity, fast response–recovery characteristics, and good reproducibility adding a high response for biological targets at the ppm and ppb levels. Table 3 describes the basics of 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides for biosensing. For example, Gao et al. [254] synthesised the 3D Cu@Cu2O nanomaterial and its biosensing properties for glucose were studied. This material is an electrically conducting material and this performance increases the electrical biosensing of glucose. The sensitive selectivity is high (195 mA M−1 cm−2), with two wide linear ranges (0.001–5.2 mM, 5.2–17.1 mM) at a working voltage of 0.6 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) in an alkaline solution. The glucose sensing of this material appeared at the low-onset potential (0.25 V) in 0.1 M NaOH solution. The 3D Cu@Cu2O nanomaterial detect the glucose by the non-enzymatic ways. This material is long-time stability, good reproducibility and high selectivity on the determination of glucose levels in (spiked) human serum samples. In other research work, the 3D Ho2O3 nanomaterials were introduced as the sensing agent for human humidity. 3D Ho2O3 nanomaterials effectively detect the moisturizing effect of skin care products, body surface humidity and human breathing vital signs. Its recovery time is 6 s and its response time is 0.3 s. It is recyclable, reproductive and stable for human humidity sensing [255].

Sun et al. [268] introduced the new 3D hierarchical plasmonic functional Cu@Co3O4@Ag nanomaterial for the detection of pesticide residue detection on fruits and vegetables. The 3D Cu@Co3O4@Ag can detect the pesticide residue of Thiram fungicide from the fruits and vegetables at 0.1 ppm concentrations. It was widely used in energy storage, wearable flexible devices, national security, disease POCT diagnosis and bioassay and other related fields. Bao et al. [270] suggested that the AChE-CS/3DG-CuO NFs are more sensitive pesticides sensing at low concentrations (ppb).

Cleaning wastewater from heavy metals is an important technique. Several industrial activities such as metallurgical applications, automobile manufacturing processes, metal smelting, fossil fuel combustion, household plumbing systems, steel manufacturing, various chemical industries, electronics production, electroplating, and electrolysis are mainly responsible for the water contamination with heavy metals [273–276]. These processes also promote chemical contaminations. The various d metals such as mercury Hg(II), lead Pb(II), copper Cu(II), chromium Cr(III), cadmium Cd(II), and arsenic As(III) are mainly heavy metals in wastewater contaminations. These heavy metals are responsible for general health concerns. For example, Hg(II) promote kidney damage and nervous system damage; Pb(II) shows kidney damage and reduced neural development; Cu(II) indicates gastrointestinal, liver or kidney damage; Cr(III) is responsible for allergic dermatitis, diarrhoea, nausea, and vomiting; Cd(II) shows the kidney damage and carcinogenic; As(III) shows the skin damage, circulatory and system issues [277]. Therefore, heavy removal is an important task in modern material and engineering sciences. Currently, there are many types of methods used to remove heavy metals from the wastewater, such as coagulation/flocculation, membrane technologies, adsorption, electrochemical treatment, remediation, ion exchange, flotation, photocatalysis and chemical precipitation [278].

Currently, the 3D nanomaterials-based 3D transition metal oxides are widely used in heavy metal removal. The reason for this is that these materials are multifunctional, more effective, recyclable, good capacity for heavy metal adsorption and have good mechanical or physical properties [279–286]. In the following research work [284], the 3D urchin-like Fe–Mn binary oxides (UFMBO) were synthesized and its adsorption capacitance for Cd(II) from wastewater was studied. It is important to note in the obtained results that its maximum adsorption capacity for Cd(II) was 74.76 mg g−1 at pH 6.0. After adsorption, the UFMBO was easily re-generated and use again effectively. Cui et al. introduced [285] new 3D porous Mn doped α-Fe2O3 nanostructures, which effectively separate the As(III), Cr(VI) and Pb(II) from the wastewater by the magnetic method. It should be noted that the adsorption capacitance of this 3D porous Mn doped α-Fe2O3 nanostructures for As(III) was higher than that for Cr(VI) and Pb(II). This is due to that the capacitance depends on the chemical and physical interactions between the metal ions and surface of nanostructure. The 3D hierarchical graphene oxide/titanium dioxide was synthesized and its adsorption capacitance for lead from waste water was investigated [281]. It is noticeable from the obtained results that the maximum adsorption capacitance of this 3D material is 199.22 mg/g for lead. The 3D nanomaterials-based 3D transition metal oxides are strongly suggested for heavy metal removal. This is due to their higher adsorption capacitance, structural properties, metal–oxygen bond nature, re-generation and cost-effectiveness [279–286].

Santhosh [280] synthesized the 3D-TiO2 (b-3D-T), magnetic 3D-TiO2: (m3D-T) and magnetic 3D-TiO2@hierarchical porous graphene aerogels (HPGA) nanocomposite by the solvothermal process. The diffuse reflectance measurement – ultraviolet (DRS-UV), Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM), transmission electron microscope (TEM), field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) were performed to analyze their chemical and physical properties. The light intensity, wavelength, photocatalyst dosage, solution pH and initial pollutant concentration of these 3D nanomaterials were studied. It is confirmed that the 3D-TiO2, magnetic 3D-TiO2 and magnetic 3D-TiO2@HPGA effectively removed the bisphenol A (BPA) and Cr(VI) from the wastewater. These 3D nanomaterials have a photocatalytic effect on the photocatalytic degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) and Cr(VI) under UV irradiation.

Organic dyes are the next important contaminants in wastewater. Every year, around a hundred thousand organic dyes were used in various industrial sections, such as leather tanning, textile industries, food processing, manufacture of rubber and plastic and paperboard. Around a hundred tons of discharged organic and inorganic dyes were disposed to the environment. Organic dyes are seriously hazardous compounds; as a result, they may be the reason for serious problems for aquatics, plants and human health. Organic dyes are non-biodegradable, and more stable at high temperatures and light exposure; these properties are also responsible for the rise of problems in the environment. Therefore, organic dye removal is crucially important in modern times. Currently, membrane separation, adsorption, advanced oxidation, and coagulation methods were used to remove the organic dyes from the wastewater [287].

The 3D nanomaterials based 3D transition metal oxides are suggested in organic dyes removal, because, of their high catalytically effects on the degradation of organic dyes. Table 4 shows various types of 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides in organic dye removal. As can be seen from Table 4 the 3D nanomaterials based 3D transition metal oxides can remove the remazol, bisphenol A (BPA), AO7 dye, methyl orange, Congo red, Rhodamine B, methylene blue, safranine T, gentian violet, fuchsin basic, and methyl orange, cationic, anionic and neutral dyes from the aqueous solutions. Their adsorption capacitance and selectivity are also high. These materials are easily regenerated after the operation and their adsorption capacitance is saved after regeneration. Some 3D nanomaterials-based 3D transition metal oxides have a high photocatalytic effect on the degradation of the above organic dyes with around 60%.

George et al. [288] prepared the CuO nanoparticles flower-like 3D nanostructures and its photocatalytic effect on the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye under visible light irradiation was investigated. In this research, the flower-like 3D CuO nanostructures were doped with transition metals (Ni, Fe and Zn). The obtained results confirmed (Fig. 9 ) that (i) Zn doped 3D materials are higher photocatalytic effect on the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye; (ii) the degradation efficiency was much higher after 60 min; (iii) the degree of degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye for Fe, Zn and Ni-doped 3D materials were accounted for 62, 63 and 52%, respectively.

It was indicated that the representation of photocatalytic degradation dye of methylene blue by the 3D Fe-doped CuO nanomaterial. It is confirmed that this nanomaterial is a semiconductor at 2.12–2.26 eV. The energy positions of 3D CuO nanomaterial were increased with the absorption of photons. The electrons in the valence band of 3D CuO nanomaterial are responsible for the absorption of photons. Then, the electrons are activated with the higher energy of photons. In the next processes, the electron-activated material reacted with the atmospheric oxygen to produce the superoxide anion (
O
2
∗
) which are very reactive. Finally, the superoxide anion reacted with the organic dye (MB) and next the water and carbon dioxide were realized [288].

The phenolic-based organic compounds are seriously dangerous to the natural environment. These compounds were released from various industrial applications such as wood, paper, pulp, paint, plastics products, resin manufacturing, coking operations, pharmaceuticals, petrochemicals and oil refining products and processes. The release of phenolic-based compounds is hazardous material for aquatic systems, animals, plants and human health. The National Pollutant Release Inventory (NPRI) of Canada and the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) confirmed the toxic properties of phenolic compounds. The phenolic-based organic compounds are mainly responsible for several serious illnesses including respiratory arrest, coma, tremors, muscle weakness and irregular breathing. The abnormal development in the offspring, growth retardation and fatal body weight reduction is appeared in the animal organism by the effect of phenolic compounds.

Therefore, phenol removal is crucially important in modern engineering and material sciences. Traditionally, the following methods were used in phenol removal: distillation, adsorption, membrane-based solvent extraction, and pervaporation, whereas liquid–liquid extraction at high phenol concentrations; wet air oxidation, reverse osmosis/nanofiltration, ozonation, solid phase extraction, photocatalytic oxidation, electrochemical, chemical and biodegradation, at low phenol concentrations. Currently, photochemical treatment, wet air oxidation, ozonation, Fenton processes and enzymatic treatment (Tyrosinases, Laccases and Peroxidases) are employed in phenolic removal [301,302].

The researchers suggested that the 3D nanomaterials-based 3D transition metal oxides are new and developed materials in phenolic compound removal. This is due to their high adsorption capacitance and photodegradation for phenol-based components. Table 5 illustrates the main details of some 3D nanomaterials based on 3D transition metal oxides in phenolic compound removal. For example, Wang et al. [303] investigated the oxidizing effect of three-dimensional Mn3O4 on phenolic degradation. The reason for this is that the more surface reactive sites and the higher specific surface area are attributed to the rise in the catalytic effect on phenolic oxidation. It is also confirmed that the catalytic stability is also high, suggesting that the hydrogen bonds between the three-dimensional Mn3O4 and phenol compounds are more stable. This 3D material chemically interacted with the phenol during the catalytic oxidation; this supports the chemical reaction between the 3D material and phenol. This interaction and the chemical reaction were activated and rose with the temperature growth. The concentration is also the next impact on the phenolic degradation, because, the inter-molecular interactions are rose with the acceleration of molecule number.

Veterinary antibiotics are common drugs; at present, it is widely used and large amounts are disposed into the wastewater. Zhao et al. [313] suggested a new method to remove veterinary antibiotics from wastewater systems. The solvothermal-hydrothermal method was applied to prepare these 3D Bi2S3/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials. This research group created new 3D Bi2S3/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials for the photodegradation of veterinary antibiotics under visible light. The obtained results confirmed that 3D Bi2S3/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials are more stable and recyclable. Its photo catalytically properties for degradation of veterinary antibiotics were regenerated with 84.3%. The degradation rate of these 3D Bi2S3/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials for veterinary antibiotics accounted for 91.4%.

Corrosion involves the degradation of metallic structures by their reaction to the environment [314,315]. It generates huge safety and economic challenges for effective and continuous industrial operations due to the replacement of corroded parts [314,315]. Corrosion is one of the biggest problems encountered in petroleum-based industries [316,317]. Corrosion is also a major concern in the acid pickling, descaling and oil-well acidification process where a huge amount of metallic components is lost in the form of corrosion. NACE estimates that the annual global cost of corrosion is nearly US $2.5 trillion equating to 3.4% of Global GDP [318]. Amongst the various methods, the use of corrosion inhibitors is one of the first and best choices for corrosion mitigation, especially in the aqueous phase. A series of organic and inorganic compounds possessing essential structural features demonstrate good anticorrosive properties [319,320]. The growing ecological cognizance and sustainable developments do not permit the use of highly effective inorganic inhibitors such as chromates, nitrates, phosphates, tungstates, molybdates etc. due to their toxicity and they have been successfully replaced by organic inhibitors. Using inhibitors along with other practices help in reducing the cost of corrosion by 15% (US $375 bn) to 35% (US $875 bn) [318,321]. Different series of organic compounds, especially heterocyclic ones, of natural or synthetic origin, are widely used [322,323]. Nevertheless, the anticorrosive use of organic compounds is also not free from drawbacks as many of them are highly toxic, relatively less effective and costly. More so, some of them are associated with the drawbacks of limited solubility and long-lasting effectivity, especially at high temperatures.

The employment of polymers, particularly in anticorrosive coatings has attracted significant attention [324–327]. Because of their polymeric nature, they deliver brilliant external coverage and defense. However, polymeric coatings do not survive in highly aggressive electrolytes for a long time due to the diffusion of corrosive species such as gases and ions through the surface micropores and crakes [327–329]. This type of diffusion destroys the coating structures completely. Therefore, the concept of self-healing i.e. self-repairing coatings is growing rapidly. The self-haling coatings possess the ability to repair surface imperfections whenever present. The self-healing coatings can be prepared by adding some additives that heal the surface damage. Metal oxides including TiO2, SiO2, CeO2, ZnO etc. are extensively used as healers in polymers coatings. Once the coating structure is damaged, the metal oxides fill the micropores and cracks and avoid the penetration of corrosion-causing species and the coating is survived for a long duration. Literature investigation suggests that MOs are extensively used as fillers or healers in different polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), polypyrrole (PPy), epoxy resin (EP or ER), polyurethane (PU) etc. based coatings. These coatings effectivity work for different metals and alloys in acidic and neutral solutions. A summary of some major reports on the self-healing behavior of MOs-based polymer coatings is presented in Table 6 .

PDMAS: Poly -dimethylamino siloxane; DGEDDS: diglycidyl ether 4, 4′- Dihydroxydiphenylsulfone; MDA: 4,4′-Methylene dianiline; NFs/SBP: Nanofibers/Schiff base phenylalanine composite; DGPMDAP: Decaglycidyl pentamethylene dianiline of phosphorus; PANI: Polyaniline; rGO: Reduced Graphene Oxide; PPy: Polypyrrole; PSS: Poly(sodium-4-styenesulfonate); BTA: Benzotriazole; PEI: polyethyleneimine; BTESPT: Bis-[triethoxysilylpropyl]tetrasulfide; 8HQ: 8-hydroxyquinoline; NIPAm: N-Isopropyl acrylamide; AA: Acrylic acid (AA); AAm: Acrylamide; QDs: quantum dots; APTAC: 3-acrylamidopropyl) trimethylammonium chloride; AMPS-Na: 2-acrylamido-2-metylpropane sulfonate sodium salt; PVA: Polyvinyl alcohol; PDMS: Polydimethylsiloxane; BPTA: N-benzylidene-5-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazol-2-amine, Ch: chitosan, PDA: polydopamine; CNTs: Carbon nanotubes; AA: Acrylic acid; AAP: Acrylic acid polymer; PVDF: poly (vinylidene fluoride); CHIT: Chitosan; CE: cerium (III) ion; MBT: mercaptobenzotiazole; EP: Epoxy; PU: polyurethane; Gr: Graphene, Gr-P: Graphene-based acrylic coating; SA: Stearic acid; PMAA:Poly(methacrylic acid)/silver nanocomposites; PEGSH-AgNPs: pPoly(ethylene glycol)thiol; PVP/PEGSH-AgNPs: poly(vinyl pyrrolidone); PDMS: Polydimethylsiloxane; ECDPA: Ethyl-2-cyano-3-(4-(dimethylamino) phenyl) acrylate; MWCNTs: Multi-walled carbon nanotubes; GO: Graphene oxide; HL: p-Phenylamine-N(4-chloro salicylaldenemine; SB: Schiff base; F-SiO2: fluorinated silica; PDMS: Polydimethylsiloxane; CRS: Cold-rolled steel; TTFOS or C8F13H4Si(OCH2CH3: Triethoxy-1H,1H,2H,2H-tridecafluoro-n-octylsilane.

It was found the key challenges and problems associated with 3D nanomaterials:
-
Synthesis challenges: Controlling the porous structure, morphology, composition, and homogeneity of 3D nanomaterials can be difficult. Multi-step syntheses are often complex. Scaling up production while maintaining consistency is an issue [500–504].
-
Limited structural stability: The high porosity makes some 3D nanostructures prone to collapse or loss of structure over time and during processing. Improving mechanical strength is an ongoing challenge.
-
Difficult characterization: The complex 3D morphology makes it hard to fully characterize the internal structure and properties using common techniques. Advanced instrumentation is required.
-
Poor processability: The porous nature and low density of 3D nanomaterials makes them difficult to process using conventional methods. Maintaining structural integrity is an issue.
-
Toxicity concerns: The high surface area and porous structure raises concerns about toxicity of 3D nanomaterials used in biomedical applications. Long-term toxicity studies are needed.
-
Difficult dispersion: Due to aggregation tendencies, dispersing 3D nanomaterials uniformly in solvents or composite materials is difficult. Surface modification is often needed.
-
Limited scalability: Scaling up controlled synthesis of tailored 3D nanomaterials from lab to industrial scale remains a barrier for commercialization.
-
Cost: Multi-step and template-based fabrication of 3D nanostructures can be expensive, limiting large scale applications. Cheaper methods are required.
-
Poor interfacial bonding: When used as fillers in composites, achieving uniform dispersion and strong interfacial bonding between the 3D nanomaterial and matrix is difficult. This limits mechanical reinforcement.
-
Limited functionality: While 3D nanomaterials provide high surface area, adding diverse functionalities to the interior and exterior surfaces in a controlled manner remains challenging.
-
Difficult modeling: The complex 3D morphology makes computational modeling of properties very difficult. Better simulation techniques are needed.
-
Unpredictable behavior: The unique nanoscale effects of 3D materials lead to unexpected or poorly understood behaviors in some applications. More research is needed.
-
Environmental persistence: The high stability and resistance to degradation of some 3D nanomaterials may lead to accumulation in the environment. Impact on ecosystems needs evaluation.
-
Reproducibility: Variability in synthetic conditions makes reproducing the exact same 3D nanostructure challenging. This can lead to irreproducible properties.
-
Limited in-situ analysis: Characterizing 3D nanomaterials under actual working conditions is difficult but critical to understand their behavior. Novel in-situ techniques are required.
-
Multifunctionality issues: While multifunctional properties are possible, optimizing and combining multiple functionalities in a single 3D platform remains difficult.

Overcoming these limitations through better synthesis techniques, improved structural stability, advanced characterization, and cost-effective processing methods remains an ongoing research challenge for the field of 3D nanomaterials. In summary, while 3D nanomaterials provide exciting opportunities, realizing their full potential requires overcoming numerous synthesis, processing, characterization, modeling, functionality, and integration challenges. More multidisciplinary research across chemistry, physics, engineering, and computational science is needed.

The contribution of 3D nanomaterials (3D NMs), especially of metal oxides, is more than 85% in nanofamility. The 3D NMs are extensively used for different applications using biomedical, energy storage, sensing and biosensing, pollutants treatment and corrosion protection. Because of the nanomeric nature, they are associated with a high surface area and high effectivity in different applications. Their sensing and biosensing applications are associated with many advantages over traditional sensors such as long-term stability, excellent selectivity, fast response–recovery speed (1–20 s), low detection limit (at ppb level), high sensitivity, recyclable, high selectivity and sensitivity. They serve as effective biosensors for different target molecules along with their sensing ability for volatile compounds (VCs) such as acetone, hydrogen sulfide, ammonia and toluene. They have also been used to sense many pesticides. They are extensively used as nanofillers in polymer-based anticorrosive coatings. Their presence enhances durability and effectivity due to their self-healing properties. They avoid the diffusion of corrosion species such as ions and gases by repairing the cracks and micropores of the damaged coating. Literature study suggests that although 3D NMs are extensively used in anticorrosive coating formulations, their use in aqueous environments is limited and should be explored. Different metal oxides including TiO2, SiO2, CeO2 and ZnO demonstrate excellent nanofilling capabilities of polymer coatings.

The 3D NMs are well-known for their heavy metal removal potential. Over traditional removers and technologies such as coagulation/flocculation, membrane technologies, adsorption, electrochemical treatment, remediation, ion exchange, flotation, photocatalysis and chemical precipitation, 3D NMs are more effective, recyclable and have better capacity. They possess the excellent capability of dye, drug and phenolic compounds removal and degradation that make them ideal candidates for wastewater and industrial influents treatments. They are widely used for electronics and energy-related applications. 3 N NMs are also used catalysts in various chemical transformations and tribological applications. However, these should be further explored, especially using their composites. The studies on impurities decontamination properties of 3D NMs should be expanded. The synthesis and consumption of 3 N NMs are associated with the use of toxic solvents and expensive catalysts therefore there is vital need to develop cost-effective and ecofriendly approach for their synthesis.
Perspective on the future of these type research.

The field of 3D nanomaterials is rapidly evolving and holds great promise for a wide range of applications. However, there are still many opportunities and challenges that need to be addressed. Here are some perspectives on the future directions of 3D nanomaterials research:

Exploring new synthesis methods: New approaches are needed to produce 3D nanomaterials with precise control over shape, composition, porosity and surface chemistry. This includes things like 3D printing, templating, self-assembly, strain engineering, and defect engineering. The goal is to enable rational design of nanomaterials tailored for specific applications.

Advanced characterization techniques: As 3D nanomaterials become more complex, advanced microscopy, spectroscopy and computational modeling tools are required to fully characterize their structures and properties. This includes things like electron tomography, X-ray nano-CT, and multiscale modeling.

Combining nanomaterials: An emerging trend is combining 3D nanomaterials with other nanomaterials (e.g. nanoparticles, nanotubes, graphene) or biomolecules to create multifunctional hybrid structures. This could enable things like targeted drug delivery, enhanced catalysis, and materials with unique optical, electronic or magnetic properties.

Real-world testing: Translation from lab to market requires extensive testing and characterization of nanomaterials under real-world operating conditions. Things like long-term stability, manufacturability, environmental/health impact, and recycling need to be addressed.

Systems integration: For applications like sensors, energy storage/conversion, and electronics, the 3D nanomaterials need to be successfully integrated into larger systems and devices. This requires optimizing interfaces and fabrication processes.

Overall, the field is still relatively young but rapidly maturing. With continued research advancing both fundamental understanding and real-world application, 3D nanomaterials have an exciting future across many technology sectors. However, close collaboration between researchers from different disciplines will be key to overcoming the remaining challenges.

Well-dispersive Pt nanoparticles grown on 3D nitrogen-and sulfur-codoped graphene nanoribbon architectures: highly active electrocatalysts for methanol oxidation. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chandrabhan Verma, Ph.D., works at the Interdisciplinary Research Center for Advanced Materials, King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals, Dhahran, 31261, Saudi Arabia. He is a member of the American Chemical Society (ACS). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis and designing of environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors useful for several industrial applications. Dr. Verma is the author of several research and review articles in peer-reviewed international journals of ACS, Elsevier, RSC, Wiley, Springer, etc. Dr. Verma received several national and international awards for his academic achievements.

Berdimurodov Elyor is an Associate professor at the National University of Uzbekistan. He has studied in the Ph.D. program at Tianjin University and the National University of Uzbekistan. He was a researcher at Tianjin University, Karshi State University, and Changchun Applied Chemistry Institute (Chinese Academy of Science). He was a participant in several international and domestic study and research programs (Chinese Government Scholarships, Chinese Great belt program, National Fundamental, and practical grants). He has published around 22 articles in Scopus and Web of science journals. H index is 7 in Scopus, 7 in Web of Science, and 11 (i-10 index of 12) in Google Scholar. He contributed over 12 book chapters He was also a participant in over 15 international conferences from China, Russian, India, and other developed countries. His research areas are corrosion science, electrochemistry, quantum chemistry, material science, green chemistry, biology, medicine, pharmacy, and nanochemistry.

Dakeshwar Kumar Verma, PhD. is an Assistant Professor of Chemistry, Govt. Digvijay Autonomous Postgraduate College, Rajnandgaon, Chhattisgarh, 491441, INDIA. His research is mainly focused on the preparation and designing of sustainable and eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors for metals and alloys those are useful for several industrial applications and material science. Dr. Verma is the author of more than 40 research papers, review articles and book chapters in peer-reviewed international journals of ACS, RSC, Wiley, Elsevier, Springer and Taylor & Francis etc. He has also worked as an editor for various books published by Elsevier and De-Gruyeter. He has total citation of more than 580 with H-index of 12 and i-10 index of 13. Recently two full time Ph.D. research scholars are working under his guidance. Dr. Verma received Council of Scientific and Industrial Research Junior Research Fellowship award at 2013.He also availed MHRD National fellowship during his Ph.D. at 2013. Recently Dr DK Verma is editing more than 04 books at the Elsevier and Dr-Gruyeter platforms

Berdimuradov Khasan works at Faculty of Industrial Viticulture and Food Production Technology, Shahrisabz branch of Tashkent Institute of Chemical Technology, Shahrisabz, 181306, Uzbekistan. His research is mainly focused on the synthesis and designing of heterocyclic organic compounds, corrosion inhibitors, corrosion protection technology and nanomaterials. He is the author of several research and review articles in peer-reviewed international journals of ACS, Elsevier, RSC, Wiley, Springer, etc. He received several national and international awards for his academic achievements.

Dr. Akram AlFantazi is a Professor of the Department of Chemical Engineering and Theme Lead (Materials and Chemistry) in the Nuclear Technology Center at Khalifa University in Abu Dhabi. He has been a professor in the Materials Engineering Department and Founding Co-Director of the Pipeline Integrity Institute (PII) at the University of British Columbia (UBC), Vancouver, Canada. He also served as the Associate Dean Research and Graduate Studies. Dr. AlFantazi obtained his Ph.D. in Metallurgical Engineering from Queen’s University, Canada in 1994. He then held a 2-year Postdoctoral fellowship at UBC. In 1996, he joined Falconbridge Ltd., where he worked as a Project Metallurgist for several years before returning to UBC in 2001, where he has been teaching and conducting research in corrosion, electrometallurgy and electrochemical processes in materials engineering. In 2006-2008, he held the Qatar Petroleum Chair in Corrosion at Qatar University. Dr. Alfantazi has more than 250 publications including 150 refereed journal publications. He has also co-edited several books. He has served as a consultant to a wide variety of companies in the chemical, metallurgical, and petroleum industries. He is a Fellow of the Canadian Institute of Mining, Metallurgy and Petroleum, NACE International, and the Engineering Institute of Canada. He regularly gives industrial short courses on corrosion and chemical metallurgy at conferences and to industry in many parts of the world. He has received a number of awards for his work including the Killam Award for Excellence in Mentoring, the Professional Engineers and Geoscientists of BC Teaching Award of Excellence, the Materials Society‘s Materials Scientists Distinguished Award and the Canadian Materials Chemistry Award. He was recently appointed a Chang Jiang Scholar at the China University of Mining and Technology.

Chaudhery Mustansar Hussain, PhD, is an adjunct professor and director of laboratories in the Department of Chemistry & Environmental Sciences at the New Jersey Institute of Technology (NJIT), Newark, New Jersey, United States. His research is focused on the applications of nanotechnology and advanced materials, environmental management, analytical chemistry, and other various industries. Dr. Hussain is the author of numerous papers in peer-reviewed journals as well as a prolific author and editor of around one hundred & fifty (150) books, books, including scientific monographs and handbooks in his research areas. He has published with ELSEVIER, American Chemical Society, Royal Society of Chemistry, John Wiley & Sons, CRC Press, and Springer.
